mission.title: Install and Configure vLLM
mission.description: vLLM is a high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for large language models (LLMs). It is ideal for applications requiring fast and scalable serving of LLMs, with support for various hardware and model types. Key trade-offs include the need for GPU resources for optimal performance and the complexity of configuration for advanced features.

steps.title: Add the vLLM Helm repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official vLLM Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration.

```bash

helm repo add vllm https://github.com/vllm-project/vllm/releases/download/v0.18.0

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the vLLM repository for version 0.18.0.
> Updates your local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install vLLM Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs)
steps.description:
This step installs the necessary CRDs for vLLM to function correctly.

```bash

kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.18.0/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

```

> This command installs the vLLM CRDs.

steps.title: Install vLLM using Helm
steps.description:
This step installs the vLLM application in the specified namespace.

```bash

helm install vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --create-namespace --version 0.18.0

```

> Installs vLLM in the 'vllm' namespace.

steps.title: Create an example Custom Resource (CR)
steps.description:
This step creates an example vLLM Custom Resource to deploy a model.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 1
EOF

```

> This command creates a sample vLLM instance using a hypothetical 'gpt-3' model.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: vllm.io/v1
kind: VLLM
metadata:
  name: example-vllm
spec:
  model: 'gpt-3'
  replicas: 1
EOF

steps.title: Verify the vLLM Custom Resource is ready
steps.description:
This step checks the status of the deployed vLLM instance to ensure it is running correctly.

```bash

kubectl get vllm -n vllm

```

> This command lists the vLLM instances in the 'vllm' namespace.

uninstall.title: Uninstall vLLM
uninstall.description:
This step removes the vLLM installation and its associated resources. Be aware that this will delete all data associated with the vLLM instances.

```bash

helm uninstall vllm --namespace vllm

kubectl delete namespace vllm

```

> This command uninstalls the vLLM Helm release.
> This command deletes the vLLM namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade vLLM to a new version
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up any critical data. This step includes draining the pods if necessary, upgrading the Helm release, and verifying the upgrade.

```bash

kubectl drain <pod-name> --ignore-daemonsets

helm upgrade vllm vllm/vllm --namespace vllm --version 0.18.0

kubectl get vllm -n vllm

```

> Replace <pod-name> with the name of your vLLM pod to safely drain it.
> This command upgrades vLLM to the specified version.
> Verifies that the vLLM instance is running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'vllm.io/v1 is not a valid API version'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the CRDs may not be installed or are outdated.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the CRDs using the command: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/vllm-project/vllm/v0.18.0/deploy/crds/vllm.io_vllms_crd.yaml

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to pull image'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This usually means the specified container image does not exist or is not accessible.

**Fix:**
Check the image name and ensure it is correctly specified in the vLLM CR.

troubleshooting.title: Pod is in 'CrashLoopBackOff' state
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This can happen if there are issues with the model loading or resource allocation.

**Fix:**
Check the pod logs with: kubectl logs <pod-name> -n vllm and ensure sufficient resources are allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Insufficient CPU resources'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that the cluster does not have enough CPU resources available for the vLLM instance.

**Fix:**
Scale up your cluster or reduce the resource requests in your vLLM CR.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new models and improved memory management features.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: No specific migration steps required for this version.

resolution.summary:
You should see output similar to the following when everything is working:

NAME               READY   STATUS    RESTARTS   AGE
example-vllm      1/1     Running   0          5m

kind: VLLM
metadata:

spec:

  replicas: 1
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster with the specified resources and the necessary tools installed.
